Now that we have matched features, we can work out how the **camera moved** between two frames.

#### Requirement: Camera calibration

To recover *real* motion we need a **calibrated** camera - one whose internal optics we've measured in advance. Calibration comes down to two things, gathered into the intrinsic matrix $K$:

- the **focal length** $f$ - how strongly the lens magnifies, i.e. how a pixel offset relates to an angle in the scene;
- the **principal point** $(c_x, c_y)$ - where the optical axis actually pierces the image (usually near the centre).

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

With $K$ known, a pixel can be turned back into a real-world direction^[An *uncalibrated* camera (no $K$) still works, but the motion can only be recovered up to an unknown distortion - real angles, shape, and scale are lost.], as visualized here:

```{=html}
<img src="assets/images/pinhole.svg" alt="Pinhole camera model: a 3D point projects through the optical centre onto the image plane; the focal length and principal point are the entries of K" style="width:100%;max-width:620px;height:auto;display:block;margin:0.8rem auto;">
```

#### How camera movement is defined

Between frame **A** ($F_{t-1}$) and frame **B** ($F_t$) the camera has moved and that motion is just two things:

- a **rotation** $R$: how the camera turned
- a **translation** $t$: which way it shifted

The scene below shows exactly that: the same camera at two positions, A and B. Drag to orbit, and move B with the sliders - the white arrow is $t$ and the white arc is $R$ (visible when you add rotation), the two quantities we're after. The **×** on each image plane marks where the *same* feature point lands in each view - that's a matched pair from the previous step.

```{=html}
<div class="demo-frame">
<iframe src="pages/html/epipolar_3d.html" width="100%" height="770" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" loading="lazy" onload="this.nextElementSibling.style.display='none'"></iframe>
<div class="demo-loading"><div class="demo-spinner"></div>loading…</div>
</div>
```

#### Packing R and t into the essential matrix E

$R$ and $t$ are usually bundled into one 3×3 matrix, the **essential matrix** $E$:

$$
E = [\,t\,]_\times \, R
$$

where $R$ is the rotation and $[\,t\,]_\times$ is the translation $t$ written as the skew-symmetric matrix:

$$
[\,t\,]_\times =
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $E$ is just a compact way of writing *where B sits relative to A*. We never measure $R$ and $t$ directly - all we have are the **×'s**, the pixel where each camera sees the same feature. A match gives a point $x$ in frame A and its partner $x'$ in frame B, and every correct match obeys one simple relation^[Here $x$ and $x'$ are *normalized* image coordinates - the pixel with the calibration divided out ($K^{-1}\cdot\text{pixel}$) - not raw pixels. Dividing $K$ out is exactly what "calibrated" buys us.]:

$$
x'^{\top}\, E \, x = 0
$$

Each match gives one such equation. And although $E$ has nine entries, only their *ratios* matter - its overall scale is unrecoverable (the same reason we can't recover the *length* of $t$) - so there are really just **eight** unknowns to pin down. That's why **eight matches** are enough to solve for $E$ [@longuethiggins1981].^[If you are curious about how $E$ is solved and why you actually only need *five matches* [@nister2004] you can check out Hartley & Zisserman, *Multiple View Geometry in Computer Vision* [@hartley2004].]

Once $E$ is known, it **splits back** into $R$ and $t$ - the motion we were after.

One catch: from a single camera the *length* of $t$ can't be recovered, only its direction - so the trajectory and map come out correct in shape but at an arbitrary overall **scale**.
